sentences.text: It was a Saturday, 11 o'clock.
That was three days-- oh, gosh.
I left two days.
That was a Thursday.
It could've been the 9th, maybe.
Three days I traveled.
Maybe it was the 9th.
It might have been even not that far.
No, sorry but--
I remembered all the dates.
But that, I only know it was Saturday.
And I'll work it out one day, what date it was.
It was November-- the beginning of November.
No, I don't think-- maybe it was earlier than November.
I wasn't nine days on the way, because I left Poland the 2nd.
And I traveled one, two, three, four.
Then in Frankfurt, under order, I had to change, stay there.
That was five.
Yes, it could have been the 9th, yes.
In Frankfurt.
Yes.
I walked outside.
And then I wasn't afraid to speak German.
I didn't have to speak a lot, but I asked which train I can get to [NON-ENGLISH], to the employment office.
And they told me number one, and how much it's going to cost-- 15 pfennig.
So I looked into my-- my pocket, and I had only 15 pfennig.
That was all I had.
Anyhow, I went to the employment office, and I told them what I want.
Is there anybody who could speak a bit of Polish?
So they found somebody who was injured in Poland, and it was two years over there.
He could speak enough for him to understand me and I understand him.
And even when he had a few words in German, here I wasn't afraid to say that I understood him.
And then I gave him the address, and could he help to find me the people?
And he told me that factory was bombed, and they were all transferred to [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said, what am I going to do over the weekend?
He said, I'll take you home.
My wife would very much appreciate a young person, because their two sons was on the eastern front.
And I said, all right.
So I was going with him in the car.
But on the way he stopped at the place.
Later on, I knew where it was.
And he wants to see a friend.
And then he came back with a very big built German man.
And he told me who he was, and would I like to stay here until Monday?
There's a little boy four years old, and somebody has to look after him because they have a young woman who was there from [NON-ENGLISH] military duty, but she has to leave today, Saturday.
Until Monday, they can't find anybody.
Would I like?
I said, how can I?
I can't speak German.
He said, not to worry.
When there will be difficulties, Hildemann will ring them up, and he will come and help me.
Over the telephone, he will help.
Somehow, later on, I got the address of the people who I had the address for.
A little bit I'm jumping over.
When they came back on Monday--
Yeah, the little boy.
And they told me to stay a little while here, would  I like?
And that little boy, Peter, he liked me, I liked him.
And he was always with me.
He taught me to speak German.
Six weeks they didn't even know that I am a foreigner.
He taught me to speak.
And he was always with me.

And then I got known from the people I was after, they went to [NON-ENGLISH]-- they told me, stay where you are, because it's much better to be in a private house than in a factory with a lot of other people.
So there, I had my own room, I had three meals a day.
They told me what to do, to clean and look after Peter, and to cook.
Mrs.
Hildemann taught me how--
Hildemann.
And they told me how to-- she told me, because every day there, there was almost the same thing during every week.
Like one day on a Tuesday, we had that.
The next Tuesday, we had the same thing, and all that.
But they had everything.
The place wasn't like it was at war.
But the bombs were coming so very often, and we had to run to shelters and all that, like bunkers.
One day I was on my bike to the eastern railway station.
There was a very, very big bunker, and I got into the tram line, and I fell over, and people were running over me.
And when I got back, I was bruised.
And you know, I said to the Hildemanns, I'm not going anywhere, because he had a small bunker under the house.
And I said, if I have to be killed, I'd rather be killed here, and not by people trampling all over me.
And they didn't disagree.
I stayed there, because when they run to some places, I had time to take down the feather beds or the blankets or Peter's toys, or whatever he wanted and all that in that bunker.
And most of all, I took down the radio.
And you know what I did?
There were other workers there, too, from other-- there was two Poles, one Russian or Ukrainian, a Frenchman and a Dutchman.
We all wanted to listen to the BBC.
And you could?
Because Hildemann wasn't there.
We closed the door, and there was really soundproof-- everything was soundproofed, and we were in seventh heaven to listen to the BBC.
They were not all the time in Polish, but we helped that-- most of all, they used to speak in German a lot to get the Germans to know what's happening, because we never knew what was happening.

And then, one day, they told me to go with them to a more-- it's a-- just only for Germans, but that was a very big-- like a little mountain, but on the mountain there was a castle, but it was in ruins.
But underneath, there was a brewery.
And in the brewery, they had rooms and places to sit and wait till the air raid was over.
And one day I went there, and that was all.
I didn't, because I wasn't allowed to open my mouth because I wasn't German.
And I said, I would rather not go because there we had to go on our bikes and all that.
I'd rather stay at home.
And in the meantime, there was a lot of air raids.
And I used to travel every evening out from the city with the boy.
They used to take me out there in the morning to come back to clean the place.
One day, when I was-- when we were all there, the place was bombed with fire bombs, and everything was burning.
But we were all under the house.
We didn't manage to get away.
The whole house and everything, he had a transport business.
And that particular Saturday, they had a lot of trucks loaded with furniture that the people wanted to get out to the country.
And they didn't manage to get away because they didn't know it was going to be such a destructive air raid.
And they couldn't even get out the next day from Frankfurt, because every road was damaged.
And before that, they told them to get out because they're going to bombard Frankfurt, but they didn't tell when.
Leaflets they threw down from a plane.
But all the civilians and the children, whatever wanted to get away to get out of Frankfurt.
But they didn't listen.
They thought it was just to frighten them or something.
And it happened.
For three days and nights, the English at nighttime, the Americans in daytime.
And then Frankfurt was-- wasn't anymore Frankfurt.
One street was left standing.
The big big blocks of houses-- it wasn't houses like units.
They were camouflaged.
They were painted like trees.
And that was-- the town was [NON-ENGLISH], and the street was called Rothschild Street.
And later on, when the Americans came, they had the headquarters, the military headquarters in Frankfurt, was in those buildings.
The last ones left, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
I survived.
Mm-hmm.
Only Hildemann was there with me.
Mrs.
Hildemann and Peter was in the country.
And after it finished, we just got out, back to the country.
We were still all in one piece.
But everything was-- somehow we got out.
And then when we came back, he started to build a house back again, but I stayed with the boy in the country.

And when we knew that it's going to-- closer and closer, because when D-Day-- you know, the 6th of June, that was '44-- when we heard about it, and I saw they came in with the newspapers, I was in the country then with all of them, and they were talking amongst each other.
And I was pretending that I'm not listening.
But I knew, the D-Day, that was the beginning of the end.
And I still can see their faces, how they looked at each other and they knew that that's no way out anymore.
And after that, he built his house up again, and he came just to travel backwards and forwards to Frankfurt.
And just before the end of the war, the Germans demolished all the bridges on the River Main.
I think there was about five, because Frankfurt was divided.
One part was called Sachsenhausen, and the other was Frankfurt [NON-ENGLISH], and passed Warsaw, like Prague.
And it was cut off, the electricity, the water.
They even managed to sink ships with food and clothing into the River Main where the port was because Hildemann was not far from the port, from the eastern port.
And then the Americans came, and they demolished the bridges because they thought they wouldn't-- they had to come through.
But the Americans came another way-- I don't know, through [NON-ENGLISH].
They didn't come through [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know which way they came.
But the 27th of April, '45, the Americans were there.
But I wasn't in Frankfurt.
I was in the country.
The country house was about maybe 20 kilometers.
But we were in the cellars for about two nights or one night and a day, because we heard the distant [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] gun-- you know, heavy artillery, the fire.
So we didn't get out.
And then in the morning, everything was quiet.
And we got out.
And while I'm talking to you, I still can see the ship with the three American soldiers on it, the first time I saw it.
They stopped, and a lot of other behind them.
One was black with the machine gun, and two were sitting in the front.
And they stepped down, and there was a lot children and all that.
They were giving us something, biscuits and chocolate.
And I was trying to share it with Peter and with other children.
And they didn't take it, because they were taught that whatever-- you know, the enemy was trying to-- that it is poisoned.
Not so, I just eat it myself first.
And when they saw that I did eat it, I didn't even remember the taste of chocolate, but it was there.
And then I went back to Frankfurt with Peter, with the little boy.
And in Frankfurt, the Americans were trying to get all the displaced foreigners from other countries to a camp.
But we were in a transit camp first.
And later on, they took us to another camp to Wetzlar.
Oh, yes, I left the Hildemann family.
I wasn't sorry.
I was sorry the night before I left, because when I realized that I'm the only one left alive, I haven't got a home, I haven't got a family, I haven't got a country.
I haven't got anything.
I haven't even got a name.
My name was wiped out.
I had a German-- [NON-ENGLISH], a working permit, and that was all-- with my photograph.
And my name wasn't on it.
It was that adopted name I had there.
And there was an incident.
One day I had to go to the police station to register myself, but that was still during the war.
And-- and I said, now, that is the end.
I haven't got any papers, and I'm at Frankfurt now.
How am I going to convince them who I am and all that?
Oh, gosh.
I don't think that anybody could believe me on that.
That night, the police station was bombed.
So instead to go there, a policeman came over to the Hildemanns And he asked me where I was born, a name and all that.
He wrote everything out.
And when I told him that I was number 20 in [NON-ENGLISH], 20, and he turned to Mrs.
Hildemann and he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
And I didn't think-- you know, do they have streets in Poland?
And I thought to myself, what did they think of Europe?
That it's just the wilderness or something?
And that is the [INAUDIBLE] folk, German, nothing else but German.
And I had to keep quiet and all that.
But later on, you know that was a miracle after miracle happened with me.
And I got through.
And then the Americans took us all together and put us in that transit camp, not far from Frankfurt.
And later on, they took us to Wetzlar.
And was at Wetzlar?
Wetzlar was a big military-- oh, how is [GERMAN] in English?
Military barracks?
Yes.
Very big buildings, four and five story buildings there.
And we were all about-- there could have been about 1,000 of us all gathered together.
Every nation was there, but not Russians.
The Russians had a separate-- they want all the Russians to go back to Russia.
And that was in May 1945?
Yes.
Yes, May.
That was May, not April anymore.
After that I stayed there for a little while.
And I got a job, and I wanted to tell the Americans who I was.
And they said, no, that's your name, and that's then.
We'll wait a while, maybe you'll find somebody who will come back, and they know you.
Then we'll be able to do something to change the name and all that.
But when I registered myself, I put my mother's maiden name, because I was afraid in Sobibor-- my mother's maiden name was Richter.
And one of the [NON-ENGLISH] in the casino, his name was Richter.
His surname was Richter.
And I got my mother's maiden name, Ordynazki.
And I registered myself with the Americans that my mother's maiden name was Ordynazki.
Ordynazki.
O-R-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] O-R-D-Y-N-A-Z-K-I.
And that was a blessing that I did that because later on, my late cousin found me because cost of that Ordynazki name.
She knew my great-grandfather's name.
Zelda, yes.
I worked in the UNRRA magazines-- you know, the United something-- I don't know how to-- but that was a displaced persons camp.
The Americans were feeding us and cladding us.
And we had everything there.
It was really, really good.
Yes.
In the beginning-- in the beginning, we were not even paid with money, only with cigarettes.
In the beginning, I was working in American-- like, it was taken over from the German hospital.
I helped out there,  and there was a lot of people who couldn't speak English.
Not English because they are American, but there was a lot amongst the Americans who could speak Polish or Russian or a little bit.
So we used to communicate with the American military because there were a lot of patients who couldn't speak English.
And we used to help them out.
And one day a soldier came along, and he had about a dozen letters from his mother in Polish, and he couldn't read Polish.
He understood.
So we sat down with him, and we read him all the letters from his mother, and then we found there were Jewish people there, too, in the American military.
And then after that, we were taken away to Wetzlar and camp.
And I started to work with the UNRRA-- with UNRRA.
And I married.
When I was in camp.
In Wetzlar.
In Wetzlar, yes.
Even before I was married, that was the beginning.
And my-- and it was so-- not tattered, but you know how worn out it was.
And it was a tweed material.
My coat, yes.
The fur collar was already given away.
We were not allowed to have anything fur during the war.
And I-- unpicked that coat, and I was going to turn it over on the wrong side.
And then I found there was something hanging in the lining of a pocket, and that was the earring, my great grandmother's earring.
And that is the diamond from that earring.
Something like this?
Can you see it?
And that was taken.
That I made from that earring, because I had only one.
I don't know what happened to the other.
Because when we were leaving home, my mother gave us, all the children, a little bit of the jewelry, whatever we didn't have.
We had to give up a lot, because the Germans had to have the first time they marched, and they wanted a kilo of gold, and five kilos of silver.
So everything had to be-- but a little bit was always, you know, left behind.
And she gave us-- you know, my sister had a ring, and I had the watch and all that.
So we all had a little bit.
And that particular earring, it was a sharp hook or something.
It got hooked on the other side of the lining.
And I didn't know about it.
And that's the only thing I have left.
Yes, yes.
The watch, I still have got that watch, yes.
So I didn't give it.
I hid it and hid it, because my parents gave it to me for my 14th birthday, and it was so precious from it.
I still have got it, but you know, the news-- and you know, it's not there anymore to wear it.
That was during the war, not in Wetzlar.
I met him during the war in Frankfurt.
He was a Polish prisoner of war in one of the oflags, the officers camp.
And he came over with two other Polish soldiers to collect coal and potatoes from the Hildemanns because he had the transport magazines.
He had a lot of things for that camp.
And I was upstairs doing something.
And then I saw three Polish soldiers, so I ran down and talked to them-- where they are, where they came from, and all that.
And then they took whatever they had to take, and they were off.
They came another time, but I wasn't there.
And when I was in that camp, before Wetzlar in that transit camp, because they were taken away from the Germans'-- the units.
And one unit was in about 50 numbers.
They were for-- for women with daughters.
There were not any men in there.
And the other unit was for single men.
And then another unit-- you know, it was-- as long as the streets the units run.
And then one day I looked down.
I was upstairs.
And next unit the soldiers were there, and that gentleman I met.
You know, and then when  we went to Wetzlar, they were transferred to Wetzlar too.
And then that was the continuation, yes.
The 24th of December, '45.
But I wasn't in the camp anymore.
I was living privately.
But I was working in camp, with the UNRRA, yes.
United Restitution Organization-- oh, help me out.
I don't know the rest.
Yes.
Andrew was born in '47 in Wetzlar.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
I even went back because the Americans wanted to have witnesses that he was a good man.
He was marvelous, that Hildemann.
But his wife was a wicked woman.
But they wanted to know--
Oh, she always used to walk around, and she couldn't stand the [NON-ENGLISH] like the foreigners.
And then it didn't matter what.
She was never happy with anything.
But he helped the foreign people.
He employed them and helped them when they were going to help out, the baker's place, like to load furniture, to get out.
He made sure that that will be loaded Saturday or Sunday.
So they will get some food from them.
And he used to try to get the Russian cards a little bit more.

And when everything was burned, I didn't have anything to wear-- I didn't even have a pair of shoes, I had only slippers-- he went and got from his Russian things, he got me shoes and he got me other things to wear because it was burned.
All I had was what I was wearing.
But my coat was still there.
I was wearing the coat.
I don't remember them, no.
No, I never made any contact.
Yes, because they were transferred somewhere else, from one factory to another.
And I used to write back to Siedliszcze, to the Golebiowskis, and to the Jagieluks, I used to write.
Can we spell Golebiowski?
Golebiowski?
Yes, that's easy.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] G-O-L-E-B-I-W-- I'll spell it again.
Ski-- that's it.
G-O-L-E-B-I-O-W-S-K-I.
But in Polish, the L is not an L with that name.
It's an 'ow,' Golebiowski.
Yes, but it's not in English.
So I did write to them, and I did write to the Jagieluks, and i used to get letters from them.
And one day she said she was in Siedliszcze, and she picked some of the [NON-ENGLISH] flowers.
And she dried them, and she sent me over.
So I had those flowers for-- I don't know for how long, till we were moving from there to there and they crumbled away.
And that was a little something.
And then after the war-- Before we go after the war, just one question about Regina, your other person, Regina.
Oh, my other person.
Mine-- that's what I just wanted to start.
My cousin was Zelda.
She was in Lvov, in Lemberg.
And she got away from Poland.
She camp to Siedliszcze and she met Mrs.
Jagieluk.
It was almost by chance.
And she told her that I survived, and she had the address from Frankfurt.
So while she was coming to Germany from Poland away, she got to Frankfurt to the Hildemanns, and the Hildemanns told her that I am in Wetzlar, in that displaced persons camp.
So she came over and tried to find me there.
And she went to the administration office, but it was a Saturday again.
But she had to go through I don't know what to tell her to look through-- she already knew, you know, not quite maybe the name or something.
But when she looked through and she said, Ordynazki, and she-- she-- that's her.
And then the way she came over, the cousin was with her.
Her cousin was with her.
They were-- he was in Warsaw, and he survived, too.
And he came in.
And it was maybe a day even before I married-- it was the day before I was going back to Poland to find somebody.
It was a Sunday.
And I was going on a Monday to Poland by train.
And he came in and he said, he's got greetings from Zelda, and all that.
I didn't recognize him right away.
He suddenly turned around in the profile.
He was 14 years when I last saw him, and he was 19, nearly 20.
And he had a big patch of gray hair because he was buried under rubbles in Warsaw, three or four, I don't know how many days.
And when he got out, his gray hair were there.
And when I recognized him, I said, Henry, it's you.
And then Zelda was outside.
She didn't want to come in to shock my straight away.
And then he called her in.
And she told me not to go home anymore.
You won't find anybody, and it will be very hard for you to get out.
And we'll see what we can do to get away, to migrate somebody.
And then I went back to the administration.
I said, now I've got a witness, somebody.
And then they helped me to change over when I got married.
The name-- I had both names on my birth certificate, because I didn't have anything with a photo.
And my working, it was like a passport, the working book, with a photo with stamps.
So I had both my names, because they needed something to go by if I am that person.
So that's when she went away to the United States because she had uncles there.
No, no, no, I was-- oh, yes, yes.
She came back.
She came back then.
And she went to Stuttgart.
And from there, she migrated to the United States.
Yes, because Wetzlar was not-- it wasn't finished then, but there was transports coming from Russia.
They were a lot of-- from Russia, Jews were coming.
It was transferred to a Jewish camp, but I stayed on there because I was sick between that, and I had to-- Andrew was a year old.
I had to go to have a kidney removed.
And instead to stay a few weeks, it took me five months to get on my feet because I had an infection.
They didn't have any penicillin, so the American army tried to get it from Geneva, and the clinic got some penicillin.
And then I got through and I was five months in hospital.
And I had more German blood than my own, because I had to have a lot of transfusions.
Then they didn't have bags of blood.
That was just the hospital staff was supplying the blood.
And then somehow I got through it.
When I got back, Andrew didn't recognize me.
He was only 13 months old.
He used to call me auntie.
But one day he looked at my face and he said, Mama.
And from that moment on, he didn't leave me.
And I wasn't even strong enough to look after him then.
But he was always with me.
And one thing he didn't like, when I was just to lie down during the night for a nap.
He used to cry his head off.
He thought at nighttime, that is enough to lie and lie.
So I used to lie on a couch.
That was all right, but not on bed.
Yes.
That's what they told me in the clinic.
Yes.
I suffered a lot with it.
But it was almost a miracle that Andrew was born and they didn't straightaway detect it.
I had the appendix removed, and it didn't make any difference.
It was still bad.
Because we tried a lot of other places.
We tried the United States, but my cousin and other friends, they couldn't get any affidavits for us.
And we needed from the American people that they will guarantee a place of stay, where to live and work.
And I applied to-- they wanted us to go to Sweden.
And I said, no, that's too close to Germany.
And then Venezuela was taken, I said, no, it's a little bit out of the-- and then all of a sudden, Australia came along.
And I learned at school a lot about Australia.
We had a map-- in the sixth grade, a map of Melbourne, because that particular year, that time, it was that Melbourne was the most modern city with straight streets.
And when we looked at that map, all the streets run-- Sydney's not like that even.
It was older.
But it wasn't the same.
And I remember even the way we pronounced-- junction.
What was it called?
[INAUDIBLE] Junction, the middle in all that.
And another thing, I saw a film.
It was called, that was about Australia.
It was still a silent film.
And then I liked where-- I don't know.
I just loved the thought to go to Australia.
And the most important thing that was the furthest away from Germany, to cross the biggest ocean.
And I made it.
The 3rd of August, 1949.
Yes, Casimir.
Cas.
Anyhow, he was Joseph because my father's name was Joseph.
And I couldn't just call him-- but his second name was Casimir.
But we shortened it for Cas.
No, no.
He wasn't Jewish.
But he anticipate in Jew-- you know, he used to go with me to the synagogue and all the celebrations, all that.
Even the rabbi used to invite us to the seder.
Because he knew I couldn't-- I'd maybe remember things from the seder, but it couldn't be the same.
He was very interested.
Oh, he was not just in Judaism but  a lot of other things.
He was like a walking encyclopedia.
He was a journalist.
So he knew a lot.
